title: Data Update 1 for 2026: The Push and Pull of Data!
gist: Damodaran opens his annual data-update series with a warning on the psychology and biases of data use, details how his aggregated industry statistics are built from a 48,156-company global sample, reports that the US market's share of global cap slipped even as tech dominates, and argues an AI 'Damodaran Bot' could eventually automate his entire data process and disrupt quant investing.

insights.claim: Data's main appeal to investors and businesses is psychological: it creates a feeling of control and a coping mechanism against uncertainty, not necessarily better decisions.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors should be wary of using data (or models) mainly to feel better rather than to genuinely reduce error.
insights.locator: The Push and Pull of Data

insights.claim: Damodaran estimates the historical equity risk premium at 7.03% (the century-long average of stock returns over treasury bonds), but with a standard error of 2.05%, implying a plausible range of roughly 4% to 11%.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Anyone plugging a single ERP number into a DCF or CAPM model is embedding several points of hidden valuation error; sensitivity-test around this wide band rather than treating 7% as precise.
insights.quote: The estimate that I will provide is 7.03% (the average over the entire period), but that number comes with a standard error of 2.05%, resulting in a range from a little less than 4% ... to greater than 11%.

insights.claim: Peer review does not make academic finance research more objective than practitioner research, because academics' incentive systems (publication, tenure) create their own biases just as compensation ties practitioner output to bias.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Discount 'peer-reviewed' academic finance claims with the same skepticism applied to sell-side research rather than treating them as bias-free.

insights.claim: Classic value-investing strategies (buying low price-to-book stocks, calling markets overpriced because P/E exceeds historical norms) rest on an assumption of mean reversion that works most of the time but fails during structural shifts that produce permanent breaks from historical valuation norms.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A market trading above historical average multiples is not automatically 'overvalued' — check whether the underlying business/earnings structure has permanently shifted before betting on reversion.

insights.claim: Despite ever-richer datasets and more powerful analytical tools, a version of Gresham's law is operating in investing and policy: bad data and misinformation drive out good data, leading to worse decisions rather than better ones.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: More data availability is not itself a solve for decision quality; investors should actively filter sources rather than assume abundance equals better signal.

insights.claim: Damodaran's practical rule for using any third-party data (including his own): check the provider's biases/priors, avoid black-box providers who won't detail methodology, and crosscheck against alternate data sources.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A concrete due-diligence checklist for vetting any data vendor or dataset before building a valuation or trading decision on it.

themes.theme: How the Damodaran Dataset Is Actually Built

insights.claim: Damodaran computes industry-level statistics like PE ratios as aggregated values (total market capitalization of all companies in a group divided by total net income of all companies, including money-losers) rather than as a simple or size-weighted average, specifically to avoid distortion from missing data (e.g. negative-earnings firms) and wide size dispersion within an industry.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Analysts pulling his industry PE/EBITDA multiples should understand they are cap-weighted aggregates, not averages of individual company ratios — using them interchangeably with a simple mean will misstate the comparable.

insights.claim: Damodaran reports industry-level rather than company-level data both because his raw data providers (S&P Capital IQ, Bloomberg) restrict redistribution of company-level data, and because individual company data is already available for free in each firm's own financial filings.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Users needing single-company figures should go to filings directly rather than expect Damodaran's public datasets to substitute for a Bloomberg/Capital IQ subscription.

insights.claim: For his global dataset, absolute-value figures (e.g. total market capitalization) are converted into US dollars, but ratio-based statistics (PE, margins, accounting returns) are currency-invariant, so a company's PE ratio would compute identically whether measured in dollars or in local currency such as Chilean pesos.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Cross-border multiple comparisons in his dataset are not distorted by FX conversion timing, unlike absolute market-cap figures which are.

insights.claim: Damodaran has argued for three decades that lease commitments should be treated as debt and R&D expenditure as capital rather than an operating expense; accounting standards adopted his lease-as-debt view in 2019, but he still diverges from GAAP on R&D and reports affected metrics (margins, accounting returns) both with and without his adjustment.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors comparing his margin/return figures to standard GAAP-based data providers should expect a persistent divergence specifically on R&D-heavy companies (e.g. tech, pharma) since he capitalizes R&D and others expense it.

insights.claim: Damodaran's 2026 global sample includes all publicly traded companies with a market price above zero — 48,156 companies total — deliberately avoiding the sampling bias created by restricting analysis to indices like the S&P 500 or to companies above a market-cap threshold, which would skew industry averages toward large-cap behavior.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: His industry averages are more representative of the full market (including small/micro caps) than benchmarks built only from large-cap indices, which is a reason his multiples can differ from S&P-500-based comparables.

insights.claim: Global equity market capitalization rose $26.3 trillion in 2025, a 21.46% increase for the year.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Sets the baseline scale of the 2025 global equity rally against which regional and sector performance below should be judged.

insights.claim: The United States remains the dominant single market with 5,994 listed firms and $69.8 trillion in market capitalization, and US stocks rose almost 16.8% in aggregate in 2025, yet the US share of total global market capitalization actually fell from 48.7% at the end of 2024 to 46.8% at the end of 2025.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A rising US market can still mean a shrinking US share of world equity value if other regions rally faster — a de-dollarization/diversification signal for global asset allocators.

insights.claim: China was the best-performing major sub-region of 2025, up almost 32.5% in US dollar terms, while India was the worst performer among major markets, up only 3.31% in US dollar terms.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A sharp reversal signal for investors who had rotated into India as the preferred emerging-market growth story over China in recent years.

insights.claim: Technology is now the largest sector globally by market capitalization, at almost 22% of total global equity value, ahead of financial services (17.51%) and industrials (12.76%); technology also delivered the highest sector return in 2025 (20.73%), while real estate and utilities were the weakest performers.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Confirms tech's continued concentration of global equity value and outperformance, reinforcing the case for watching sector-concentration risk in global cap-weighted indices.

themes.theme: AI Disruption of Data Work and Quant Investing

insights.claim: Damodaran believes his own 'Damodaran Bot' — an AI trained on everything he has published — could already do every mechanical step of his annual data-collection and processing workflow better and faster than he can, and he plans to hand more of next year's data update process over to it, with the eventual goal of having it take over almost the entire process.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A leading valuation practitioner conceding that AI can fully replace his own core data workflow is a strong signal for how far AI automation of routine financial-analysis labor has progressed.
insights.quote: As I looked at the process that I went through to put my datasets together, I realized that there was no aspect of it that a bot cannot do better and faster than I can.

insights.claim: Businesses whose core model is collecting and processing data and charging high prices for that service are exposed to AI-driven disruption unless they can find other sources of differentiation.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A direct warning applicable to data/analytics vendors (financial data providers, research shops) about business-model risk from AI substitution.
insights.locator: I do think that there is a message here for businesses

insights.claim: Quant investing strategies built around large teams of quantitative analysts crunching big datasets for excess returns will become more challenging, not easier, as AI proliferates in that space — a contrarian view relative to the common assumption that AI simply supercharges quant investing.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Implies alpha from data-crunching quant strategies may compress as AI commoditizes the analytical edge, pushing durable alpha toward judgment/narrative-based approaches instead.
insights.quote: To the extent that a great deal of quant investing has been built around smart numbers people working with large datasets to eke out excess returns, it will become more challenging, not less so, with AI in the mix.

insights.claim: Damodaran's own defense against being replaced by AI is to lean into multi-disciplinary work that combines numbers with narrative and requires intuition, imagination, and generalist judgment — areas he believes bots cannot yet replicate — rather than competing on mechanical, rule-based tasks.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A template for any knowledge worker facing AI substitution risk: shift value-add from mechanical data processing toward narrative synthesis and judgment calls that don't reduce to rules.
insights.locator: In 2024, I talked about the Damodaran Bot
